claimed_contribution: Introduces a first test of neural processes involved in making and updating other-directed online recommendations in response to peer recommendations.
Combines two previously separate literatures - neural correlates of successful recommendation/idea propagation and neural correlates of social influence - in one paradigm.
Presents an fMRI App Recommendation Task designed to mimic key features of new media recommendation environments and to examine both average effects and individual differences in susceptibility.
context: The study is set in an online word-of-mouth recommendation context using mobile game apps, a fast-growing app market category. Male adolescents made recommendations based on real iTunes-style app information while exposed to peer recommendation feedback.
design_and_data: A behavioral pilot (N = 106 undergraduates) first tested whether peer feedback changes recommendation updating in a computer-based App Recommendation Task. The main study used fMRI with 65 male adolescents (ages 16-17) after exclusions; participants rated 80 puzzle game apps before scanning, then rerated them in the scanner after manipulated peer feedback (higher, lower, same, or not rated; 20 trials each). During each fMRI trial, participants saw their initial rating reminder, peer feedback, and then gave a final rating. Analyses included repeated-measures ANOVAs for behavior, whole-brain contrasts (uncorrected p = .001), and ROI-based regressions relating neural activity during differing feedback to each participant's overall tendency to change recommendations.
focal_constructs: valuation system activity (ventral striatum, orbitofrontal cortex)
main_findings: Participants changed recommendations significantly more when peer feedback differed from their initial recommendation than when feedback was the same or absent, in both pilot and fMRI samples.
Within subjects, when conflicting peer feedback was followed by recommendation change (vs no change), activity was higher in ventral striatum and orbitofrontal cortex.

Between subjects, greater right TPJ activity during conflicting peer feedback was associated with greater overall susceptibility to change recommendations; other hypothesized ROIs were not significant in this analysis.
one_line_summary: Using an fMRI app-rating task, the paper shows that adolescents' online recommendations shift toward peer opinions behaviorally, with valuation-related activity tracking within-person updating and right TPJ activity tracking between-person susceptibility.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior social influence and communication neuroscience work by moving from self-oriented preference conformity to other-directed recommendation decisions in an online-like setting. It links valuation-system findings from conformity studies with TPJ-based mentalizing findings from successful idea propagation research.
research_gap: Prior neuroimaging studies had examined either social influence on personal preferences or neural correlates of successful recommendation transmission, but not the neural processes involved in making and updating other-directed recommendations in response to peer recommendations. The authors also note a lack of such evidence in adolescents in new media recommendation environments.
research_question: How does peer recommendation feedback influence adolescents' final recommendations to others in an online-like word-of-mouth context? Which neural processes are associated with recommendation updating when peer opinions conflict with one's own, and which neural signals explain individual differences in susceptibility to such influence?
